:::{#thm-g-computation-consistency}

#### Consistency of g-computation

Suppose the data $(Y_i, A_i, Z_i)$, $i = 1, \dots, n$, are i.i.d. draws from the population;
suppose the outcome regression is correctly specified,
so the fitted coefficients are consistent,
$\hat\beta \overset{p}{\to} \beta^*$
(standard MLE consistency for a correctly specified GLM; see @agresti2015foundations, Chapter 4);
suppose the identification assumptions of @thm-causal-marginal-risk hold;
and suppose the predictor vectors are uniformly bounded,
$\|x_{a,i}\| \le B < \infty$ (as holds when covariates are bounded).
Then the g-computation estimator (@def-g-computation)
is consistent for the causal marginal risk $\pi_a$ (@def-causal-marginal-risk):

$$\hat\pi_a \overset{p}{\to} \pi_a$$

::: {.proof}

Split the estimator into the average of the **true** conditional risks
plus an estimation-error term:

$$
\ba
\hat\pi_a
&= \frac{1}{n}\sumin \hat\pi(a, z_i)
&& \text{[definition of the estimator]}
\\
&= \frac{1}{n}\sumin \teal{\pi(a, z_i)}
+ \frac{1}{n}\sumin \left(\hat\pi(a, z_i) - \teal{\pi(a, z_i)}\right)
&& \text{[add and subtract the true risks]}
\ea
$$

The estimation-error term converges to zero in probability.
By hypothesis the fitted coefficients are consistent,
$\hat\beta \overset{p}{\to} \beta^*$.
Write the fitted and true risks as the logistic link of the linear predictor,
$\hat\pi(a, z_i) = \expitf{x_{a,i}'\hat\beta}$
and $\teal{\pi(a, z_i)} = \expitf{x_{a,i}'\beta^*}$,
and recall that $\expit$ is Lipschitz with constant $\tfrac{1}{4}$
(its derivative $\expitf{u}\,(1 - \expitf{u})$ is at most $\tfrac{1}{4}$).
Then

$$
\ba
\left|\hat\pi(a, z_i) - \teal{\pi(a, z_i)}\right|
&= \left|\expitf{x_{a,i}'\hat\beta} - \expitf{x_{a,i}'\beta^*}\right|
&& \text{[risk as logistic link of the linear predictor]}
\\
&\le \tfrac{1}{4}\left|x_{a,i}'\hat\beta - x_{a,i}'\beta^*\right|
&& \text{[$\expit$ is $\tfrac{1}{4}$-Lipschitz, by the mean value theorem]}
\\
&= \tfrac{1}{4}\left|x_{a,i}'(\hat\beta - \beta^*)\right|
&& \text{[factor out $x_{a,i}'$]}
\\
&\le \tfrac{1}{4}\,\|x_{a,i}\|\,\|\hat\beta - \beta^*\|
&& \text{[Cauchy–Schwarz]}
\\
&\le \tfrac{B}{4}\,\|\hat\beta - \beta^*\|
&& \text{[boundedness hypothesis $\|x_{a,i}\| \le B$]}
\ea
$$

uniformly in $i$, giving

$$
\frac{1}{n}\sumin \left|\hat\pi(a, z_i) - \teal{\pi(a, z_i)}\right|
\le \tfrac{B}{4}\,\|\hat\beta - \beta^*\| \overset{p}{\to} 0
$$

(a uniform bound that pointwise convergence of the individual terms,
correlated through the shared $\hat\beta$, would not provide on its own).
The remaining term averages the true risks over an i.i.d. sample
$z_1, \dots, z_n$ from the marginal distribution $f_Z$,
so the weak law of large numbers gives

$$
\frac{1}{n}\sumin \teal{\pi(a, z_i)}
\overset{p}{\to} \E{\teal{\pi(a, Z)}}.
\qquad \text{[weak law of large numbers]}
$$

Finally, @thm-causal-marginal-risk identifies this expectation
with the causal marginal risk:

$$
\E{\teal{\pi(a, Z)}} = \pi_a.
\qquad \text{[causal marginal risk identification]}
$$

Combining the three steps, $\hat\pi_a \overset{p}{\to} \pi_a$.

:::

:::
